title: 4 Tahsas — St. John the Apostle & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "4 Tahsas — St. John the Apostle & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"4 Tahsas — St. John the Apostle & The Assembly

narrative\": \"On the fourth day of Tahsas the Church honors the holy Apostle and Evangelist John, the Beloved Disciple, whom the Ethiopian tradition calls Yohannes, derived from the Hebrew Yochanan, meaning 'the Lord is gracious.' Of all the apostolic writers, John penetrated most deeply into the mystery this month proclaims, for it was he who opened his Gospel with the sublime declaration: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God'; and it was he who pronounced the theological culmination of all Christian preaching: 'And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory.' John, who rested his head upon the breast of Christ at the Last Supper, was entrusted with the care of the Virgin Mother from the cross, and was granted the apocalyptic visions of Patmos, stands in this month of the Incarnation as the supreme theological witness to the event Tahsas shall celebrate.

Alongside the beloved apostle, the Church commemorates on this day the Great Martyr Barbara, whose name is derived from the Greek barbaros, signifying one who speaks an alien tongue — yet she, the daughter of a pagan nobleman of Heliopolis or Nicomedia, heard within herself the voice of the one true God and turned from the idols of her father with such ardor that she commanded the builders of her bath-house to insert three windows instead of two, that the Trinity might be honored in the light that illumined her contemplation. Her father denounced her to the magistrate and with his own hand beheaded her; for this deed he was immediately struck by lightning, a sign the fathers read as the divine judgment upon those who extinguish the light of truth in their own households.

The virgin martyr Juliana of Nicomedia stands beside Barbara in this day's assembly, she who likewise refused marriage to a pagan prefect and was subjected to terrible torments before her beheading. Juliana's name descends from the Latin Julianus, bearing the connotation of youthful vigor; and indeed her faith displayed the vigor of one who had grasped the eternal youth of the Gospel against the senescence of the dying pagan world. The pairing of these two noble virgins with the Apostle John, who was himself preserved virginal in the ancient tradition, forms a constellation of witnesses to the purity of heart through which God is seen.

The theological seal of this fourth day of Tahsas is the mystery of illumination through darkness. John beheld the glory of the Word in the beloved face of Jesus; Barbara sought the light of the Trinity in the three windows of her contemplation; Juliana endured the darkness of torment rather than surrender the lamp of faith. All three testify to the paradox at the heart of the Incarnation: that the Light of the world enters the darkness of human flesh precisely in order that the darkness might comprehend it, and that those who receive Him are given power to become the children of that Light.
